---
title: "Ch 3 — Understanding Quadrilaterals"
subtitle: "Four sides, one closed shape — and a whole family of names"
lesson-id: "cbse8.understanding-quadrilaterals"
tier: "cbse8"
ncert-class: 8
ncert-chapter: 3
ncert-chapter-title: "Understanding Quadrilaterals"
board-exam: false
prerequisites: ["t1.shapes-angles"]
unlocks: ["cbse8.mensuration", "cbse9.quadrilaterals", "cbse9.triangles"]
applied-domains: ["game-dev", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 35
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest before you begin.

1. Can you tell a triangle from a quadrilateral on sight?
2. Do you remember that the angles of a triangle add up to $180^\circ$?
   If not, revisit
   **[Shapes and Angles](../../tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd)**.
3. Can you measure an angle with a protractor?
4. Do you know what *parallel lines* means — lines that never meet?

If three were a confident yes, read on.
:::

## Touch

Cut four sticks from a sheet of stiff paper or use four pencils. Lay
them on a table, end to end, so they form a closed loop with four
corners. (Tape the corners loosely if you can — pinholes work.)

Push gently on one corner. Watch what happens. Push on the opposite
corner. Watch again.

```{ojs}
viewof angle = Inputs.range([20, 160], {value: 90, step: 1, label: "Bottom-left angle (degrees)"})
viewof side = Inputs.range([60, 180], {value: 120, step: 5, label: "Slant length"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const a = (angle * Math.PI) / 180;
  const b = side;
  const w = 200;
  const x1 = 50, y1 = 200;
  const x2 = x1 + w, y2 = y1;
  const x3 = x2 + b * Math.cos(Math.PI - a), y3 = y2 - b * Math.sin(a);
  const x4 = x1 + b * Math.cos(a), y4 = y1 - b * Math.sin(a);
  const angles = [a, Math.PI - a, a, Math.PI - a].map(r => Math.round((r * 180 / Math.PI) * 10) / 10);
  const sum = angles.reduce((s,x)=>s+x,0);
  return html`<svg width="380" height="240" style="background:#fafafa;border:1px solid #ddd;">
    <polygon points="${x1},${y1} ${x2},${y2} ${x3},${y3} ${x4},${y4}"
      fill="#bde0fe" stroke="#1d4ed8" stroke-width="2"/>
    <text x="190" y="225" text-anchor="middle" font-family="system-ui" font-size="13" fill="#444">
      angle sum &asymp; ${sum.toFixed(1)}&deg;
    </text>
  </svg>`;
}
```

Move both sliders. The shape changes — it becomes a tall slanted box,
then a squashed one — but watch the angle sum at the bottom. Does
*anything* about it stay constant?

## Wonder

Sit with these.

1. As you slid the corner, the four angles each changed. Did their
   *sum* change?
2. Two opposite sides of your loop look parallel. Does the loop *have*
   to have parallel sides for the corners to form?
3. If two adjacent sides are equal in length, what does the shape start
   to look like?
4. Could a four-sided closed shape have a *reflex* angle (more than
   $180^\circ$) at one corner?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice (don't peek until you've thought)

The angle sum stays at $360^\circ$ no matter how you push. The chapter
is built around that fact, plus a tree of special quadrilaterals — each
defined by which sides or angles are made equal or parallel.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Cut a square along a diagonal

Take a paper square and cut it along one diagonal. You now have two
triangles. What can you say about each of them?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Are the triangles right-angled? Are they identical?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The cut goes corner to corner, so each triangle has the right-angle
corner of the square. The two legs are equal (sides of the square). The
two triangles are **congruent right isoceles triangles**. Each has
angles $90^\circ, 45^\circ, 45^\circ$.

The angles of the *square* split as: at the cut corners, $90^\circ =
45^\circ + 45^\circ$. At the uncut corners, the $90^\circ$ stays
intact. Sum: $90 + 45 + 45 + 90 + 45 + 45 = 360^\circ$.
</details>

### Problem 2 — Cut any quadrilateral

Now do it again with a *random* (non-square) four-sided paper shape.
Cut along one diagonal. You get two triangles. What is the sum of *all*
the angles in those two triangles?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Each triangle's angles add to $180^\circ$. There are two of them.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$2 \times 180^\circ = 360^\circ$. Now: those six triangle-angles
together account for the four corners of the original quadrilateral —
each quadrilateral corner is split into two pieces (or stays whole) by
the diagonal.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Two triangles together have $2 \times 180 = 360^\circ$ of angle. When
you reassemble into the quadrilateral, those angles regroup into the
four corner angles. So the **angle sum of any quadrilateral is
$360^\circ$**.
</details>

### Problem 3 — A missing angle

A quadrilateral has angles $80^\circ$, $90^\circ$, $110^\circ$, and a
fourth that you don't know. Find the fourth angle.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Use the fact you just proved.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$80 + 90 + 110 + x = 360 \Rightarrow x = 80^\circ$.
</details>

### Problem 4 — Spot the parallelogram property

Draw a parallelogram (a four-sided shape with both pairs of opposite
sides parallel). Measure both pairs of opposite *sides*.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
You don't need to measure to predict.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Opposite sides of a parallelogram are **equal in length** as well as
parallel. (Reason: drawing a diagonal makes two congruent triangles by
ASA — alternate interior angles plus the shared diagonal.)
</details>

### Problem 5 — Diagonals of a rhombus

A rhombus has all four sides equal. Draw both diagonals. What do you
notice about them where they cross?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Look at the angle they make. And the lengths from the crossing point to
the corners.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The diagonals of a rhombus **cross at right angles** and **bisect each
other**. (Each diagonal divides the rhombus into two congruent triangles
which are mirror images.)
</details>

### Problem 6 — Exterior angles

You walk around a closed quadrilateral path, turning at each corner.
You return to your start. Through what total angle have you turned?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Walking around any closed shape and ending up where you started, facing
the same way, your total turn is one full circle.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$360^\circ$. The sum of the *exterior* angles of any convex polygon —
not just a quadrilateral — is $360^\circ$.
</details>

You have just discovered the angle sum, the parallelogram side property,
the rhombus diagonal property, and the exterior-angle fact — the four
load-bearing facts of the chapter.

::: {.fun-fact}
**Euclid's *Elements*** (c. 300 BCE), the most-printed mathematics
textbook in history, classified quadrilaterals in Book I, Definitions
22 — *square, oblong* (rectangle), *rhombus, rhomboid* (parallelogram),
and *trapezia* — almost the same family tree you just met. Two thousand
three hundred years later, the names live on; the only major change is
that "trapezium" in modern Indian and British usage means "at least one
pair of parallel sides", whereas Euclid's *trapezia* meant "no pairs
parallel". The geometry, though, is unchanged.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
A quadrilateral is **two triangles glued along a diagonal**. That single
move explains everything: angle sum is $2 \times 180^\circ = 360^\circ$,
parallelograms split into a pair of congruent triangles, rhombi into
four right triangles, and rectangles into two equal right triangles.
Whenever you are stuck on a quadrilateral problem, draw the diagonal —
the triangles you already know will do the work.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A **quadrilateral** is a closed plane figure bounded by four line
segments.

**Angle-sum property:** the four interior angles of any quadrilateral
add to $360^\circ$.

**Family tree** (each name adds one more constraint):

- **Trapezium** — at least one pair of parallel sides.
- **Parallelogram** — both pairs of opposite sides parallel; opposite
  sides equal; opposite angles equal; diagonals bisect each other.
- **Rectangle** — parallelogram + all angles $90^\circ$; diagonals
  equal.
- **Rhombus** — parallelogram + all sides equal; diagonals perpendicular
  and bisect each other.
- **Square** — rectangle + rhombus; all of the above at once.
- **Kite** — two pairs of adjacent sides equal; one diagonal bisects
  the other at $90^\circ$.

:::

A short verse, in the spirit of Bhāskara's *Līlāvatī*:

> *Four-sided closed — three-sixty in all;*
> *Both pairs parallel, sides match, angles match, diagonals halve.*
> *Square the prince of the family — every property at once;*
> *Rhombus the dancer — perpendicular cross at the heart.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> In a parallelogram $ABCD$, $\angle A = 70^\circ$. Find the other
> three angles.

Competent solver's path:

1. Opposite angles of a parallelogram are equal: $\angle C = \angle A =
   70^\circ$.
2. Adjacent angles are supplementary (they sit on the same side of a
   transversal cutting two parallel lines): $\angle B = 180 - 70 =
   110^\circ$.
3. By the same rule, $\angle D = 110^\circ$.

Check: $70 + 110 + 70 + 110 = 360^\circ$. ✓

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Three angles of a quadrilateral are $60^\circ, 70^\circ, 130^\circ$.
Find the fourth.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$60 + 70 + 130 + x = 360 \Rightarrow x = 100^\circ$.
</details>

**W2.** True or false: every square is a rectangle.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**True.** A rectangle requires four right angles; a square has them
plus equal sides. Every square satisfies the rectangle definition.
</details>

**W3.** True or false: every rhombus is a square.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**False.** A rhombus needs only equal sides; its angles need not be
$90^\circ$. A square is a rhombus, but not every rhombus is a square.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** In a parallelogram $PQRS$, $\angle P = 3\angle Q$. Find all four
angles.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Adjacent angles of a parallelogram sum to $180^\circ$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\angle P + \angle Q = 180$ and $\angle P = 3\angle Q$.
$3\angle Q + \angle Q = 180 \Rightarrow \angle Q = 45^\circ$,
$\angle P = 135^\circ$. Opposite angles equal: $\angle R = 45^\circ$,
$\angle S = 135^\circ$.
</details>

**P2.** A rhombus has a diagonal of length $24$ cm and the other of
length $10$ cm. Find the side length.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The diagonals bisect at right angles. Each side is the hypotenuse of a
right triangle with half-diagonals as legs.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Half-diagonals: $12$ cm and $5$ cm. Side $= \sqrt{12^2 + 5^2} = \sqrt{144
+ 25} = \sqrt{169} = 13$ cm.
</details>

**P3.** The exterior angle at one vertex of a regular hexagon is what?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Sum of exterior angles is $360^\circ$, divided equally among 6 corners.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$360 / 6 = 60^\circ$.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** In quadrilateral $ABCD$, $\angle A : \angle B : \angle C :
\angle D = 1 : 2 : 3 : 4$. Find each angle and identify any pair of
parallel sides if you can.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Total $= 1+2+3+4 = 10$ parts. Total angle $= 360^\circ$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Each part $= 36^\circ$. Angles: $36^\circ, 72^\circ, 108^\circ, 144^\circ$.

Adjacent pairs: $\angle A + \angle B = 108$, $\angle B + \angle C =
180^\circ$, $\angle C + \angle D = 252$, $\angle D + \angle A = 180^\circ$.

Two adjacent sums equal $180^\circ$: that means $BC \parallel AD$
(co-interior angles supplementary). It is a **trapezium**.
</details>

**S2.** A kite has two pairs of consecutive equal sides $AB = AD = 5$
cm and $CB = CD = 12$ cm, with $\angle B = \angle D = 90^\circ$. Find
the length of diagonal $AC$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Triangle $ABC$ is right-angled at $B$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$AC = \sqrt{AB^2 + BC^2} = \sqrt{25 + 144} = \sqrt{169} = 13$ cm.
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**1-mark (objective).** Sum of the interior angles of a polygon with 7
sides is
(a) $720^\circ$ (b) $900^\circ$ (c) $1080^\circ$ (d) $540^\circ$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Formula: $(n-2) \times 180^\circ$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(7-2) \times 180 = 5 \times 180 = 900^\circ$. **Answer: (b).**
</details>

**2-mark (short answer).** Two adjacent angles of a parallelogram are in
the ratio $2:3$. Find each angle of the parallelogram.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Adjacent angles sum to $180^\circ$. Parts: $2+3 = 5$. Each part
$= 36^\circ$. Angles: $72^\circ, 108^\circ, 72^\circ, 108^\circ$.
</details>

**3-mark (short answer II).** $ABCD$ is a parallelogram. The bisectors
of $\angle A$ and $\angle B$ meet at $P$. Show that $\angle APB =
90^\circ$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\angle A + \angle B = 180^\circ$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
In triangle $APB$: $\angle PAB = \tfrac{1}{2}\angle A$ and $\angle PBA =
\tfrac{1}{2}\angle B$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\angle PAB + \angle PBA = \tfrac{1}{2}(\angle A + \angle B) =
\tfrac{1}{2}(180^\circ) = 90^\circ$.

In triangle $APB$, angles sum to $180^\circ$:
$\angle APB = 180^\circ - 90^\circ = 90^\circ$. ✓
</details>

**4-mark (long answer).** $ABCD$ is a quadrilateral with $\angle A =
100^\circ, \angle B = 80^\circ, \angle C = 100^\circ$. (i) Find $\angle
D$. (ii) Show that $AB \parallel DC$. (iii) What special quadrilateral
is $ABCD$ at minimum?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Use the angle sum, then look for supplementary co-interior pairs.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(i)** $\angle D = 360 - (100 + 80 + 100) = 80^\circ$.

**(ii)** Consider sides $AB$ and $DC$ with transversal $BC$. The
co-interior angles are $\angle B = 80^\circ$ and $\angle C = 100^\circ$,
summing to $180^\circ$. So $AB \parallel DC$.

**(iii)** Consider sides $AD$ and $BC$ with transversal $AB$.
Co-interior angles $\angle A + \angle B = 180^\circ$ as well. So $AD
\parallel BC$.

Both pairs of opposite sides are parallel — $ABCD$ is at minimum a
**parallelogram**.
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Exemplar Problem 3.4 (angle bisectors in a
parallelogram) is a near-twin of the 3-mark item.
**Previous-year pointer:** CBSE 2018 Set 1, Q11 ("if three angles of a
quadrilateral are given...") matches the 4-mark item closely.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"A square is not a rectangle."** It is. The hierarchy is inclusive
   — every square satisfies the rectangle definition (four right
   angles).
2. **"All quadrilaterals with equal diagonals are rectangles."** No.
   An isoceles trapezium also has equal diagonals. The full criterion
   for a rectangle is *parallelogram with one right angle* (or
   *parallelogram with equal diagonals*).
3. **"Diagonals of a kite bisect each other."** Only one of them gets
   bisected (by the axis of symmetry). The other does the bisecting.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **A [quadrilateral](/glossary.qmd#quadrilateral) is a closed plane
   figure with four sides — and the four interior
   [angles](/glossary.qmd#angle) always sum to $360^\circ$, no matter
   the shape.**
2. **The family is a hierarchy: trapezium $\subset$
   [parallelogram](/glossary.qmd#parallelogram) $\subset$ rectangle
   $\cap$ rhombus $=$ square. Each name adds one more constraint on
   sides or [angles](/glossary.qmd#right-angle).**
3. **When stuck, draw a diagonal. It splits any quadrilateral into two
   [triangles](/glossary.qmd#triangle) — and the triangle properties
   you already know will solve the problem.**

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you draw the family tree of quadrilaterals from memory and say
   what extra constraint each name adds?
2. Did you solve at least 5 of the 6 *Discover* problems and 5 of the 7
   *Practice* problems without peeking past Hint 1?
3. Given a quadrilateral with three known angles or two known sides,
   can you immediately spot which property to invoke?

- **3 yes:** move on to **[Data Handling](../04-data-handling/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo *Stretch S1* and the 4-mark board item, then move on.
- **0–1 yes:** rebuild from *Discover*. The angle-sum proof in
  Problem 2 is the foundation of everything else.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[Shapes and Angles](../../tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd)** — angles, parallel lines, the $180^\circ$ sum in a triangle.

### What this unlocks

- **[Mensuration](../09-mensuration/index.qmd)** — areas of trapezia, parallelograms, rhombi.
- **[Class 9 — Quadrilaterals](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/08-quadrilaterals/index.qmd)** — proves the side and diagonal properties rigorously.
- **[Class 9 — Triangles](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/07-triangles/index.qmd)** — congruence rules underpin the parallelogram proofs.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 8, Chapter 3 — Understanding Quadrilaterals** (NCERT 2023
  rationalised syllabus). Covers Sections 3.1–3.4: polygon angle sums,
  the family tree, properties of parallelograms.
- **Sister synthesis lesson** (different pedagogical angle): no direct
  synthesis lesson at this level; the closest non-CBSE treatment of the
  same material lives in tier 3 geometry, but it assumes a fuller proof
  background. Stay with this chapter and the NCERT textbook.
- **Read alongside the NCERT textbook**, which remains the canonical
  source for the board paper.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Every flat surface in a 2D game — platforms,
tile maps, UI panels — is a rectangle or quadrilateral mesh.
Collision detection between rotated rectangles uses the diagonal and
side properties of this chapter.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** Tessellations of the plane by quadrilaterals are at the
heart of finite-element methods, the numerical workhorse for solving
partial differential equations. The mesh quality is judged by how
close each cell is to a square — the prince of the family.
:::
